evidence: Package layout is clearly domain-aligned: api, config, exploration, indexer/blacklab, model, query, utils, viz — each with a coherent scope and no obvious mis-placements.
Dependency flow is largely top-down: api → exploration → query → model; config is a shared value layer imported by api, exploration, and query. No import cycles were found.

RelationType enum (model package) conflates two distinct roles: structural query strategy discriminators (SURFACE, DEP) and semantic relation categories (ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF). The enum is consumed by SketchHandlers (structural dispatch), CollocateProfileComparator (semantic lookup), and GrammarConfigLoader (parsing) — three different semantic contexts with no enum-level documentation distinguishing the two namespaces.

All six api handler classes import com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpExchange — a JDK internal API in the com.sun namespace. While bounded to the api package, this creates a codebase-wide coupling to an unsupported API that could be removed or restricted in future JVM versions (e.g., via --illegal-access restrictions or module encapsulation).

SemanticFieldExplorer acts as a thin façade over CollocateProfileComparator and MultiSeedExplorer. The façade is intentional, documented, and provides value by offering a unified API to the exploration subsystem.
WordSketchApiServer constructs and wires all handler objects in its constructor — hub-like, but this is framework-required composition. The constructor is 2-overload with clear injection seam.
LongIntHashMap in utils has no cross-module imports and represents a well-contained low-level utility.
BlackLabConllUIndexer and ConlluConverter in indexer/blacklab form an appropriately isolated subpackage with no upward imports into the api or exploration layers.

Package layout maps cleanly to domain capabilities: model (data), config (grammar definitions), exploration (semantic field algorithms), query (corpus execution), api (HTTP layer), indexer (corpus indexing), utils (shared primitives), viz (rendering).
model/package-info.java documents the naming convention split between record types (no get prefix) and JavaBeans classes (get prefix) — demonstrates intentional ownership of conventions.

API layer is cleanly decomposed by concern: SketchHandlers (word sketch queries), ExplorationHandlers (semantic field endpoints), ConcordanceHandlers (concordance lookup), CorpusQueryHandlers (raw BCQL), VisualizationHandlers (SVG rendering) — each handler class has a coherent responsibility cluster.
ExploreResponseAssembler is a dedicated translation layer in api, keeping ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult free of presentation concerns.

SemanticFieldExplorer correctly acts as a public facade over the package-private CollocateProfileComparator and MultiSeedExplorer — new engineers can understand the exploration subsystem through a single entry point.
GrammarConfig (data) and GrammarConfigLoader (loading) are separate classes with distinct roles, so callers that only need the loaded data don't carry a reference to the I/O machinery.

ComparisonResult Javadoc says it is 'Produced by CollocateProfileComparator#compareCollocateProfiles' — but the public API surface is SemanticFieldExplorer#compareCollocateProfiles. The internal routing is leaking into the model documentation, undermining the facade abstraction.

RelationType enum in model carries both structural query strategy values (SURFACE, DEP) and semantic category values (ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF) without documented separation — creates a blurry contract at the model boundary.
SingleSeedExplorationOptions and ExplorationOptions are cleanly factored records with clear documentation of the single-seed-only parameter (nounsPerCollocate).

ExplorationOptions Javadoc refers to package-private class names (SemanticFieldExplorer#exploreMultiSeed) using code-comment-style links rather than stable API references — minor but creates fragile docs.
Java naming conventions are highly consistent throughout: PascalCase classes/records, camelCase methods/fields, ALL_CAPS constants — no snake_case intrusions.

All API handler classes are package-private (no public modifier): ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ExploreResponseAssembler, HttpApiUtils, SketchHandlers, VisualizationHandlers — consistent visibility boundary across the api package.

The model package explicitly documents its mixed record/class strategy in package-info.java: records (AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun) omit get-prefix per Java record convention; traditional classes (ComparisonResult, ExplorationResult) use get-prefix per JavaBeans convention. This split is intentional and documented.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti calls parseBaseExploreRequest twice (once explicitly before validateExploreRequest, which calls it again). The first call is for an early-exit check on 'nouns_per', while other handlers that do not need this check go directly through validateExploreRequest. This is a behavioral deviation from the sibling handler pattern.

Main.java handleBlackLabIndexCommand, handleBlackLabQueryCommand, handleBlackLabServerCommand — the 'BlackLab' prefix is consistent across the three subcommand handlers, even though the user-facing commands are 'blacklab-index', 'blacklab-query', and 'server' (the third drops the 'BlackLab' prefix). This inconsistency between the user command name and the private method name is minor but creates a navigation mismatch.
ExplorationOptions and SingleSeedExplorationOptions are records in the exploration package — consistent with the model package's record usage for value objects.

validateExploreRequest returns @Nullable ValidatedExploreRequest: the null-return-as-error pattern is used consistently within ExplorationHandlers but diverges from the IAE-throw pattern used by requireParam in HttpApiUtils.
HttpApiUtils Javadoc comment references 'CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN' (a constant name) but the actual implementation is getCorsAllowOrigin() (a method) — stale reference in documentation.
convention_outlier.issues_preventing_higher_score: The dual-parse in handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti creates a behavioral outlier versus sibling handlers; the stale CORS constant name reference in Javadoc is a minor doc drift.

evidence: HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling establishes a uniform error mapping for all API handlers: IllegalArgumentException → 400, IOException → 500, any other Exception → 500. This is applied to every route registration in WordSketchApiServer.

HttpApiUtils.requireParam throws IllegalArgumentException when a required parameter is missing or empty — the exception propagates up and is caught by wrapWithErrorHandling, resulting in a 400 response. This is a consistent pattern across all handlers.

ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig uses a null-return pattern to signal a 400 error: it sends the error response itself and returns null, requiring all callers to check for null and return early. This diverges from the IAE-throw convention used by requireParam and creates a mixed error strategy within the same class.

SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries catches IOException per-relation and accumulates errors in a list rather than propagating them. Failed relations appear in the response as an 'errors' array. This is an intentional partial-failure design but the semantics (success code 200 with embedded error list) are not documented on the method.
BlackLabQueryExecutor wraps BlackLab exceptions as IOException with descriptive messages — context is preserved at the adapter boundary.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern throws IllegalArgumentException for invalid relation config parameters (relationType absent, unsupported POS group) — consistent with the IAE-for-validation pattern.

LogDiceCalculator.compute returns Double.NaN (UNCOMPUTABLE sentinel) rather than throwing IllegalArgumentException when headwordFreq <= 0 or collocateTotal <= 0. This is the only place in the codebase where a computation-boundary error uses a sentinel return value instead of an exception. The Javadoc documents this but it is inconsistent with the IAE style used at other computation boundaries.
GrammarConfigLoader throws IOException for all config errors (missing file, invalid JSON, missing fields) — consistent, with descriptive messages and clear wrapping.
ConlluConverter leaves the in-progress sentence open on the error path (documented by comment), ensuring clean failure detection. The try-with-resources guarantees flush/close even on error.
Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand catches IOException from grammar config loading and prints an actionable error before returning — appropriate CLI-layer error boundary.

Main.requireNextArg calls System.exit(1) on missing argument — documented as CLI-only behavior, but mixing exception-style error handling (IAE in the library) with exit-style handling in the same codebase is a minor strategy inconsistency.

API handler method names are descriptive and action-oriented: handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, handleSemanticFieldComparison, handleSemanticFieldExamples, handleConcordanceExamples — all reveal intent clearly.

validateExploreRequest parses, resolves, and constructs a ValidatedExploreRequest record — it does more than 'validate', which implies a boolean/void contract. The name partially misleads about the return value.
resolveRelationConfig is accurate — it looks up a RelationConfig from the grammar config by ID, sending a 400 if absent. 'resolve' correctly implies lookup-with-fallback semantics.
buildBaseExploreResponse is accurate — it constructs the shared fields of an explore response map.

round2dp in ExploreResponseAssembler is an abbreviation inconsistent with the codebase's use of full English words (formatSeedCollocates, buildEdges, populateExploreResponse). 'roundToTwoDecimalPlaces' or 'round2Decimals' would be more consistent.

CollocateProfileComparator.buildRawAdjectiveProfiles — 'raw' is mildly ambiguous. It means 'un-scored, un-profiled' at this stage but this is not self-evident from the name alone. 'collectPerNounAdjectiveScores' or 'fetchAdjectiveScoresPerNoun' would be more explicit.

MultiSeedExplorer.explore is overly generic — it is the single entry point of the class, but 'explore' reveals nothing about the multi-seed nature of the computation. 'computeMultiSeedCollocateIntersection' or 'exploreSeeds' would be more precise.

SemanticFieldExplorer.deriveNounCqlConstraint — 'derive' is slightly weak for what is actually 'resolve a CQL pattern from grammar config with a hardcoded fallback'. 'resolveNounCollocatePattern' would be more precise.
HttpApiUtils.requireParam — 'require' is a clean, established pattern for mandatory-parameter-or-throw semantics. Acceptable.
ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions — clear, domain-appropriate names.
CollocateQueryHelper — 'Helper' suffix is a mild code smell but the class has a defined responsibility (collocate search, scoring, ranking logic for the executor) so it is not a pure dumping ground.
ValidatedExploreRequest — accurate and communicates that validation has already occurred.
SeedCollocateData in MultiSeedExplorer — accurate record name for its local use.
fetchExamples in SemanticFieldExplorer — clear verb-noun naming.
identifyCoreCollocates — clear intent.

QueryExecutor interface has a single production implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor). The interface Javadoc explicitly acknowledges this and states it acts primarily as a testability seam. This is honest and the deferred-split rationale is documented.

SemanticFieldExplorer is a facade over CollocateProfileComparator and MultiSeedExplorer. The facade provides genuine value: it presents a unified API, validates pre-conditions, and handles the 'which implementation' decision for callers. Not a pass-through wrapper.

Edge.toMap() in the model package serializes itself into a plain Map for JSON output. This presentation concern belongs in ExploreResponseAssembler, not in a model value object. The method is called from ExploreResponseAssembler.buildEdges via a stream map — moving the logic there would not require changing callers outside the assembler.

RelationConfig builds CQL patterns (buildFullPattern, buildCollocateReversePattern) and infers the collocate POS group from the pattern string (computeCollocatePosGroup). A config/value object is doing structural analysis that is closer to parsing logic — these responsibilities sit with the data but are not data.

ExplorationHandlers.parseBaseExploreRequest is a one-liner that delegates entirely to HttpApiUtils.parseQueryParams. The method name differs from the underlying method but adds no behavior, policy, or error handling — a pass-through wrapper that exists only to give a local name.

HttpApiUtils is a utility class combining HTTP response writing and request parsing. The Javadoc explicitly justifies the co-location (both serve a single concern — HTTP layer of the REST API — and are used exclusively within the api package). The justification is sound; this is not a low-cohesion dumping ground.

WordSketchApiServer.wrapHandler delegates directly to HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling with no added logic. The method comment says it exists to enable unit testing, but the actual testability benefit is on wrapWithErrorHandling itself — wrapHandler is a pure pass-through.
GrammarConfigLoader/GrammarConfig separation is well-fitted: GrammarConfigLoader handles I/O and parsing, GrammarConfig is the data value object. No abstraction fitness issue here.

CqlUtils is a focused utility with cohesive methods (splitCqlTokens, substituteAtPosition, mergeLemmaConstraint, extractConstraintAttribute, escapeForRegex) — all closely related CQL string manipulation operations. Not a dumping ground.
LongIntHashMap is a custom data structure with a clear performance rationale — memory-efficient long→int map. Justified abstraction.
CollocateQueryHelper encapsulates collocate search, scoring, and ranking logic — cohesive helper with a defined responsibility cluster. Not a generic util.

pom.xml declares a focused, purposeful dependency set: blacklab-engine and blacklab-query-parser (corpus engine), fastjson2 (JSON), slf4j-api + logback-classic (logging), jspecify (nullability annotations), JUnit 5 (testing). No overlapping libraries for the same purpose.

BlackLab 4.0.0 is resolved from a GitHub raw-content URL (https://raw.githubusercontent.com/instituutnederlandsetaal/BlackLab/mvn-repo/) rather than Maven Central. The pom.xml explicitly documents this risk with a multi-paragraph comment: rate-limiting, branch deletion, and repo-rename scenarios are called out, along with two mitigation options (local install script, private registry). This is honest risk documentation but the fragility itself is a real concern for CI environments.

blacklab-engine likely pulls in Apache Lucene as a transitive dependency, which the project appears to use directly via BlackLab's API (BlackLabIndex, Hits, etc.). If Lucene classes are used directly in CollocateQueryHelper or BlackLabQueryExecutor, there is an implicit version coupling to whatever Lucene version BlackLab bundles.
No duplicate JSON libraries: fastjson (v1) is not present alongside fastjson2. No Gson, Jackson, or org.json alongside fastjson2.
No duplicate logging frameworks: slf4j-api as facade, logback-classic as runtime implementation — standard pairing with no competing logging backends.
jspecify (1.0.0) for @NonNull/@Nullable annotations — lightweight, no runtime overhead, no duplication with other null-safety libraries.

com.sun.net.httpserver is used as the HTTP server layer rather than adding a framework dependency (Netty, Undertow, Jetty). This is a pragmatic choice that keeps the dependency footprint minimal, at the cost of using an internal JDK API.

No lock file is present (Maven uses pom.xml version pins directly; no Maven wrapper lock equivalent). Versions are pinned explicitly in properties: fastjson.version, junit.version, etc. — good practice for reproducibility.
blacklab-engine at version 4.0.0 is a major version of a domain-specific engine. The project depends on two BlackLab artifacts (engine + query-parser) at the same version — consistent co-versioning.
dependency_health.issues_preventing_higher_score: The BlackLab GitHub raw-content repository URL is a CI reliability risk. If the branch or URL changes, the build breaks silently. The install-blacklab.sh fallback is documented but not the default path.

evidence: MultiSeedExplorer.explore has a clearly named four-phase structure (fetch, identify, build, core) with each phase delegated to a focused private helper. The main method reads as a composition of collaborators.
CollocateProfileComparator.compareCollocateProfiles has a clean three-phase structure (buildRawAdjectiveProfiles, buildAdjectiveProfileList, sort) with no interleaved concerns.

ExploreResponseAssembler methods (formatSeedCollocates, formatDiscoveredNouns, formatCoreCollocates, formatAdjectiveProfile) are consistently extracted at the same granularity — balanced extraction with no micro-fragmentation.
LongIntHashMap is a careful open-addressing hash map with linear probing, sentinel values, and load-factor-triggered rehashing. The implementation is direct, correct, and well-commented.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore unpacks ValidatedExploreRequest field-by-field into local variables (seed, resolvedConfig, relationType, topCollocates, minShared, minLogDice, nounsPerCollocate) before use. This adds 7 variable declarations before any logic. The record fields could be accessed inline or destructured into fewer named groups.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti calls parseBaseExploreRequest once for the nouns_per early-exit check, then validateExploreRequest which calls parseBaseExploreRequest again — redundant parsing of the query string on every request to this endpoint.

ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig sends a 400 HTTP response and returns null on error — mixing side effects (HTTP response) into a method that returns a value. Callers must do null-check-and-return, which creates repetitive control flow at three call sites.

SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries uses BiFunction<RelationConfig, ExecutedSketch, Map<String,Object>> as a callback — the lambda in handleFullSketchForType is straightforward but the callback pattern for what is essentially a response-format switch adds indirection.

ConlluConverter.convertConlluToWplChunks has complex state management with 5 mutable variables (sentences, tokens, chunks, inSentence, sentencesInChunk) and a try-within-try-with-resources pattern. The comment explains the subtle error-path behavior. The logic is correct but dense.
Main.showUsage is a sequence of 30+ System.out.println calls — verbose but this is standard for CLI usage text.
SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern (the package-private overload with 5 parameters) is a moderately complex orchestration method but the logic is sequential and well-commented with phase markers.
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens has good examples in its Javadoc and the implementation cleanly handles nested brackets and quoted strings.

The api → exploration → query layering is clean and one-directional. ExplorationHandlers calls SemanticFieldExplorer, which delegates to CollocateProfileComparator and MultiSeedExplorer, which call QueryExecutor. Each boundary is explicit.

ExploreResponseAssembler is the single translation layer between model objects (ExplorationResult, ComparisonResult) and JSON-ready maps. Model-to-presentation translation happens in one obvious place.

ExplorationOptions and SingleSeedExplorationOptions cleanly represent the input contract at the api→exploration boundary. SingleSeedExplorationOptions.toBaseOptions() provides a clean downward projection.

ExplorationResult is reused for both single-seed and multi-seed exploration results, but the semantics of the seed field differ: in single-seed mode it holds one lemma; in multi-seed mode it holds a comma-joined string of seeds (e.g. 'theory,model,hypothesis'). This implicit format contract at the boundary is not documented anywhere and callers that read result.getSeed() get different data shapes.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed delegates almost immediately to MultiSeedExplorer.explore after validation — a thin layer that adds only a pre-condition check. This is appropriate given the documented intent (facade over two collaborators).

handleSemanticFieldComparison does not validate that seeds.size() >= 2 at the handler level. The validation is inside SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles which throws IAE. The 400 error message will reference 'compareCollocateProfiles requires at least 2 seed nouns' — an internal method name exposed to API clients.

The three-argument CollocateProfileComparator.compareCollocateProfiles(Set, double, int) receives parameters destructured from ExplorationOptions at the SemanticFieldExplorer boundary: opts.minLogDice(), opts.topCollocates(). This means the options object is constructed in the API layer, threaded through the facade, then immediately destructured into primitives at the implementation level — the options abstraction doesn't fully survive the boundary.

model.ComparisonResult docs say it's produced by CollocateProfileComparator#compareCollocateProfiles (internal), while the public contract is SemanticFieldExplorer#compareCollocateProfiles. This documentation seam mismatch was noted in high_level_elegance; from mid_level_elegance it means the model's stated origin and actual API origin diverge.
CorpusQueryHandlers.handleCorpusQuery reads the request body, parses JSON, extracts the 'query' field, and delegates to executor.executeBcqlQuery. The boundary handoff is explicit and minimal.
ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples extracts params, delegates to the executor, and formats results — clean three-step handoff with no glue accumulation.
VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial reads and parses the JSON body, constructs RadialPlot.Item list, calls RadialPlot.renderFromItems — the boundary between HTTP parsing and rendering is clear.

SemanticFieldExplorer class Javadoc provides a worked example of the house exploration algorithm with concrete input/output trace — this is genuinely informative documentation that reveals non-obvious algorithmic behavior.
HttpApiUtils class Javadoc contains a multi-paragraph justification for co-locating two concerns in one class — this is insightful documentation explaining a design decision, not a restatement.
GrammarConfigLoader methods have thorough Javadoc (parseRelation, validatePositions, deriveTokenPosition) — all comments explain WHY, not just WHAT.

ConlluConverter has a meaningful comment explaining why the in-progress sentence is left open on the error path: 'writing a partial </s> tag would produce a malformed chunk that is harder to detect than an abrupt EOF' — genuine non-obvious constraint documentation.
LogDiceCalculator class comment includes the formula: 'logDice = log2(2 * f(AB) / (f(A) + f(B))) + 14' — appropriate domain reference.

ExplorationHandlers.parseBaseExploreRequest Javadoc: 'All four exploration handlers share this identical preamble.' This is inaccurate (only two handlers call it directly; two use validateExploreRequest which calls it internally) and the comment restates structure rather than adding insight.
GrammarConfig accessor Javadoc: '@return the version string' on getVersion() is a restating comment — the method name and return type already communicate this fully.
GrammarConfig.getRelation: '@return Optional.ofNullable(relationsById.get(id))' — the implementation is trivially readable from the code; the Javadoc adds nothing.

RelationEdgeType enum Javadoc on toString(): 'Returns the stable label, identical to label().' — this restates what label() says. The explanation about why toString() is overridden (logging frameworks emit API-safe label) is genuinely useful, but the opening sentence is redundant.
No noisy entry/exit debug logging on normal methods — logger.debug is used sparingly and meaningfully (exploration milestones, progress counters).
No defensive null checks on non-nullable typed values — jspecify @NonNull/@Nullable annotations are used to document nullability contracts without generating redundant defensive checks.
No pass-through wrapper functions that add no logic (except parseBaseExploreRequest, noted above under abstraction_fitness).
No identical boilerplate error handling copied verbatim across files — wrapWithErrorHandling centralizes the error handling pattern.
ai_generated_debt.issues_preventing_higher_score: A small cluster of restating Javadoc (getVersion, getRelation, toString-opens-with-restatement) and one inaccurate structural comment (parseBaseExploreRequest 'all four handlers') prevent a higher score.

evidence: CorpusQueryHandlers.java lines 93-97: deprecated 'limit' alias for 'top' is still active code: `if (top == null) top = obj.get("limit") != null ? obj.getIntValue("limit") : null; // @deprecated alias`
CorpusQueryHandlers.java line 44 Javadoc: 'The deprecated "limit" field is also accepted for backward compatibility.'
Comment on limit alias: 'Planned removal: remove the limit alias in v2.0 or whenever breaking API changes are acceptable.' — removal is deferred indefinitely.

RelationConfig.java lines 163-166: 'tag=' attribute name supported as legacy synonym for 'xpos=' in grammar patterns; comment states 'Grammar files using tag= should migrate to xpos='; no known active grammar files use tag= but the code path is live.
Recent renames are fully propagated: ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, CorpusQueryHandlers, ExploreResponseAssembler all reflect the new names with no old-name usages.
Deprecated constructors for SemanticFieldExplorer/CollocateProfileComparator have been removed — no old constructors remain.

The /api/bcql HTTP endpoint path was not renamed (still 'bcql') even though the handler class was renamed to CorpusQueryHandlers — the endpoint URL is an external API contract so this is appropriate, but the mismatch between URL and class name is a navigation friction.

Directory structure: api/ (9 files), exploration/ (5 files), model/ (10 files), query/ (5+ files), config/ (4 files), indexer/blacklab/ (2 files), utils/ (3 files), viz/ (1 file), collections/ (0 files, empty).
Empty 'collections/' package exists under word_sketch root — no Java files present, no documented purpose.

ExploreResponseAssembler.java lives in api/ but its responsibility is model-to-presentation translation: it imports AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExplorationResult, RelationEdgeType from model/ and converts them into JSON-ready maps. This is a presentation concern, not a handler concern.

api/ package contains both HTTP infrastructure (WordSketchApiServer, HttpApiUtils) and handler classes (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) and response assembly (ExploreResponseAssembler). Three distinct sub-concerns co-located.

model/ package has 10 files covering DTOs (Edge, ExplorationResult, ComparisonResult), value objects (AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, RelationEdgeType), and type enums (PosGroup, RelationType) plus package-info — the enum types (PosGroup, RelationType) are also imported by config/ and query/ which creates a reasonable dependency direction.

Coupling direction is clean: api/ imports exploration/, model/, query/, config/; exploration/ imports query/, model/, config/; config/ imports model/, utils/; query/ imports utils/. No circular coupling detected.
Recent refactoring correctly moved QueryResults to query/ and RadialPlot to viz/ — these are well-placed.

HttpApiUtils.parseQueryParams Javadoc has an empty description body — only a @throws clause is present. The @param for 'query' is absent, leaving the nullability contract undocumented at the method signature level.

Main.requireNextArgOrExit is named requireNextArg but calls System.exit(1) on invalid input — the 'require' naming convention in the codebase (see HttpApiUtils.requireParam) implies an exception, not process termination. The Javadoc does warn about this, but the name misleads callers scanning the call-site.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti calls parseBaseExploreRequest twice (lines 118 and internally via validateExploreRequest at line 61) — the rawParams check is performed on one parse, then silently discarded; validateExploreRequest re-parses the same URI. The effective contract (rawParams is checked then re-parsed) is not documented.
parseBcqlRequest in CorpusQueryHandlers returns null for 413 and throws IAE for 400-class errors — this dual mechanism is explicitly documented in the Javadoc, making the contract honest.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern (package-private overload): Javadoc clearly states it is a 'package-private testing seam' and production code must use the public overload — the contract distinction is explicitly documented.

QueryExecutor interface: each method has a complete Javadoc with @param, @return, @throws, including the note that findCollocations 'returns an empty list silently if null or empty' — matches the implementation.

ExplorationHandlers.validateExploreRequest returns @Nullable with the convention documented: null means a 400 has already been sent and the handler should return. This is an unusual contract but is explicitly stated in the Javadoc.
CollocateQueryHelper constructor accepts @Nullable BlackLabIndex with a Javadoc explaining the testing seam — honest contract.

SemanticFieldExplorer.identifyCoreCollocates: `totalScore / Math.max(1, freq)` — freq is drawn from collocFrequency which is only incremented for nouns in discoveredNouns. The entry is only added to coreCollocates when `freq >= minNounsForCore` which is `Math.max(2, discoveredNouns.size() / 3)`, so freq is always >= 2 at this point. The `Math.max(1, freq)` guard against division-by-zero is unreachable.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti: query string parsed twice — line 118 calls parseBaseExploreRequest(exchange) into rawParams for a nouns_per guard check, then line 126 calls validateExploreRequest(exchange) which internally calls parseBaseExploreRequest(exchange) again. The first rawParams map is discarded.

BlackLabQueryExecutor.buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution: the else branch after throw is structurally unnecessary — `if (startsWith) return ...; else throw ...` is equivalent to `if (startsWith) return ...; throw ...`. Not wrong but adds a syntactic else that is never reached after the throw.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern: `if (seed == null || seed.isEmpty()) { return ExplorationResult.empty(seed != null ? seed : ""); }` — inside the if-body, when seed is null, the outer condition is already true, so `seed != null ? seed : ""` correctly handles both null and empty cases. This is NOT dead logic.
MultiSeedExplorer.identifyCommonCollocates: `int threshold = Math.min(minShared, seedCount)` followed immediately by the filter — clear and correct.

handleSemanticFieldExplore: `String seed = req.seedsRaw()` takes the seeds parameter as a raw (unsplit) string and passes it directly as a single seed to exploreByPattern. If a client passes 'theory,model', the combined string is used as the seed lemma. The behavior is well-defined (corpus won't match the multi-word string) but the lack of validation or splitting could silently return empty results for multi-value inputs.
Java's static type system enforces return types at compile time — no annotation-lie return types are possible across the codebase.

ExploreResponseAssembler.formatAdjectiveProfile uses magic string literals for category: `String category = adj.isFullyShared() ? "fully_shared" : adj.isPartiallyShared() ? "partially_shared" : "specific"`. The three string values duplicate the semantics of AdjectiveProfile's boolean methods but have no enum backing — a change to one would not be caught by the compiler.

All handler classes build JSON responses as Map<String, Object> — the standard Java pattern for dynamic JSON output, but it means response shapes cannot be statically verified. A missed key or wrong value type only manifests at runtime.

@Nullable / @NonNull annotations from jspecify are used in 3 locations (CollocateQueryHelper constructor, ExplorationHandlers.validateExploreRequest return type, QueryExecutor.executeDependencyPatternWithPos) but absent from most other public API parameters — inconsistent nullability documentation.
QueryResults contains nested records (WordSketchResult, CollocateResult, ConcordanceResult, SnippetResult) — all well-typed with explicit field types.
RelationEdgeType, RelationType, PosGroup enums are used consistently throughout; no raw string or integer comparisons bypass them in production code.
GrammarConfig, RelationConfig are final/record-like value objects with explicit types — no Object or untyped collections as fields.

CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates accepts `Map<String, String> posMap` — the caller passes `Collections.emptyMap()` when POS is unavailable. The empty map is an acceptable type-safe sentinel but `Optional<Map<String,String>>` would be more expressive of optionality.

Flagged boilerplate at ExplorationHandlers.java:3, CollocateProfileComparator.java:9, SemanticFieldExplorer.java:9 — confirmed as package/import block Java boilerplate (Logger declaration + SLF4J imports). This is structural Java boilerplate that cannot be extracted; it is not a design issue.

ExplorationHandlers has 4 handler methods but three of them share a validate→dispatch pattern (validateExploreRequest) and one (handleSemanticFieldComparison) does its own validation manually. The fourth handler (handleSemanticFieldExamples) does its own parameter parsing too. Three distinct validation styles in one class.

handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti parses the query string via parseBaseExploreRequest into rawParams for a nouns_per check, then immediately calls validateExploreRequest which calls parseBaseExploreRequest again — a design flaw where the pre-check bypass path (rawParams) is not integrated into the shared validation flow.

SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries uses BiFunction and Predicate parameters to unify two structural paths (surface vs. dependency) — a well-designed, data-driven solution that replaces what could have been duplicated loops.

SemanticFieldExplorer is a clean facade: it delegates to MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator, and the internal exploration algorithm. Each class has a focused responsibility. The three-way delegation is clear.

ExplorationHandlers.ValidatedExploreRequest private record captures validated state cleanly, avoiding parameter telescoping in handler methods. The pattern is used consistently for 2 of 4 handlers (the explore and explore-multi paths) but not for comparison or examples.
CollocateProfileComparator and MultiSeedExplorer are both package-private and accessed only through SemanticFieldExplorer — the facade boundary is enforced by visibility.
CorpusQueryHandlers.parseBcqlRequest cleanly separates parsing/validation from execution (handleCorpusQuery). The handler is focused and small.

HttpApiUtils.getCorsAllowOrigin() reads System.getProperty('cors.allow.origin', 'http://localhost:3000') at call time (not class-load time) — the property can be changed between calls. This is a deliberate design: the Javadoc explicitly states 'Read at use time via getCorsAllowOrigin() so that the system property is evaluated when the response is sent, not at class-load time.'
Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand reads System.getProperty('grammar.config', 'grammars/relations.json') at server startup time — appropriate for a CLI entry point.

BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor opens BlackLabIndex eagerly: `this.blackLabIndex = BlackLab.open(new File(indexPath))`. This is a resource acquisition, not a hidden side effect — the constructor declares IOException and the caller manages lifecycle.
SemanticFieldExplorer constructor creates CollocateProfileComparator and MultiSeedExplorer eagerly — deterministic initialization order with no external dependencies.

WordSketchApiServer constructor builds all handler instances but defers port binding to start() — construction and network initialization are explicitly separated (documented in the constructor Javadoc).
GrammarConfigLoader is a static utility class with no instance state — no initialization order concerns.
LongIntHashMap initializes with DEFAULT_CAPACITY = 16 and LOAD_FACTOR = 0.75f as static final constants — pure data, no side effects.
No global mutable singletons found in production code — all state is instance-scoped and constructed through the call chain from main().

Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand uses AtomicReference<WordSketchApiServer> serverHolder to register a shutdown hook before server construction, ensuring cleanup even if construction throws — the initialization sequence is explicit and safe.
No circular initialization dependencies: config/ does not depend on api/, query/ does not depend on exploration/, etc.
initialization_coupling.issues_preventing_higher_score: The getCorsAllowOrigin() pattern reads a system property on every response — while intentional, it means the CORS origin can change between requests in the same JVM run, which may be unexpected in production. This is a minor semantic concern, not a correctness bug.

evidence: 27 test classes for 39 production files — good ratio. 230 tests pass per task description, with 9 skipped due to live-index dependency.

Live-index tests are in 5 files: BlackLabQueryExecutorTest, BlackLabBcqlConcordanceTest, BlackLabDummyTest, DependencySketchTest, BlackLabConllUIndexerTest. All skip via Assumptions.assumeTrue when the index directory is absent — these tests validate the BlackLabQueryExecutor execution path but only when a local corpus index is available.
SemanticFieldExplorerInternalTest and SemanticFieldExplorerTest use StubExecutor (mock) to test exploration logic without an index — good unit isolation.
HandlersTest has 25 @Test methods and uses a hand-rolled MockExchange to test HTTP handlers — covers 400/200 response codes for most handler paths.
WordSketchApiServer (route registration, start(), stop()) has no dedicated test class — the server binding, route registration, and OPTIONS handling are not directly exercised in CI.
VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial HTTP path is not covered by HandlersTest — RadialPlotTest covers the SVG rendering logic only, not the HTTP handler entry point.
CorpusQueryHandlers.handleCorpusQuery is not explicitly tested in HandlersTest (no test method name visible for BCQL endpoint).
HybridAlgorithmTest and CollocateQueryHelperTest use mock/stub BlackLabIndex subclasses to test ranking and scoring logic — coverage of logDice and collocate ranking paths is present.
No snapshot tests found — zero snapshot overuse.

Test fragility: live-index tests depend on local file system state (index directory existence) via environment variable CONCEPT_SKETCH_TEST_INDEX — CI builds silently skip these without a failure signal.

REST parameter naming inconsistency: explore and explore-multi endpoints use 'seeds' (plural) but /api/concordance/examples uses 'word1' and 'word2' — two different conventions for referencing lemmas in the same API family.

handleSemanticFieldExplore uses the 'seeds' parameter name but accepts a single unsplit string as the seed lemma — the plural name implies comma-separated multi-seed input, creating a naming mismatch with the single-seed contract.

explore: seeds=house (single); explore-multi: seeds=theory,model,hypothesis (comma-separated multi); /api/semantic-field: seeds=theory,model (comparison). All three use the same parameter name 'seeds' with different cardinality semantics.

/api/semantic-field endpoint path does not indicate 'comparison' mode in the URL — the endpoint name implies semantic field overview, but it actually computes pairwise collocate profile comparisons across seeds.

Java QueryExecutor interface: findCollocations(lemma, cqlPattern, minLogDice, maxResults) and executeSurfacePattern(lemma, bcqlPattern, minLogDice, maxResults) have identical signatures but different pattern format requirements. The difference (cqlPattern must start with '['; bcqlPattern uses labeled positions) is documented but the caller must know which method to use based on pattern semantics, not the type system.

SemanticFieldExplorer public methods: exploreByPattern(seed, relationConfig, opts) takes RelationConfig; exploreMultiSeed(seeds, relationConfig, opts) takes RelationConfig; compareCollocateProfiles(seedNouns, opts) does NOT take a RelationConfig — the comparison path uses a hard-coded adjective pattern from grammar, not a caller-specified relation.
handleSemanticFieldExamples uses 'adjective' and 'noun' parameter names — completely different from 'seeds' used by all other exploration endpoints, even though it operates on the same domain.
REST API mixing of query parameters: /api/sketch/{lemma} takes the lemma in the URL path; all exploration endpoints take seeds as query parameters — two conventions for the main noun/lemma input.
WordSketchApiServer.start() binds to InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress() — the server only accepts connections from localhost. This is a network-level access control applied uniformly to all endpoints.
No authentication middleware, session validation, or authorization checks are present on any route handler — all endpoints are consistently unauthenticated.
All route handlers are registered with the same registerHandler/registerGetHandler/registerPostHandler mechanisms — no per-route auth decorator present or absent on any subset.

POST /api/bcql accepts arbitrary BCQL queries and executes them against the index. Input is size-limited (65536 bytes) and complexity-limited (1024 chars, 20 bracket tokens). No auth check, but loopback binding limits caller trust boundary.
No RLS, no permission strings, no service-role bypass patterns — all database access is via BlackLab index which has no user-level access control concept.
CORS is handled uniformly via HttpApiUtils.setCorsHeader(exchange) called on every response — no per-endpoint CORS policy differences.
The /health endpoint is the only explicitly 'public' endpoint but all endpoints are equally public — no documentation distinguishing authenticated vs. unauthenticated endpoints.
No user data is stored or processed — the API operates on a static read-only corpus index. Authorization is not applicable to the data model.
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: The server lacks any explicit documentation that all endpoints are intentionally unauthenticated and loopback-only, and the /api/bcql query endpoint accepts arbitrary corpus queries without rate limiting — in a networked deployment (if loopback restriction were ever lifted) this would be a security gap.

strengths: Package layout matches domain capabilities: api, config, exploration, query, model, indexer are all predictably scoped.
No circular import dependencies found across any package boundary.
The api package is self-contained with all handlers package-private and only WordSketchApiServer as the public entry point.
GrammarConfig/GrammarConfigLoader separation cleanly isolates the loaded value object from the loading machinery.
BlackLab adapter code is isolated in indexer/blacklab with no leakage into exploration or api layers.
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: The primary architectural tension is a single enum (RelationType) that silently conflates two orthogonal namespaces — structural query strategy and semantic category — causing usage patterns that are semantically inconsistent across consuming classes.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates solid layer discipline and predictable dependency direction, earning a score well above the midpoint. The RelationType enum mixing and the com.sun.net.httpserver coupling are genuine boundary concerns that prevent a higher score — the former because it blurs the model contract, the latter because it anchors the entire API layer to an unsupported JDK namespace.

strengths: Package decomposition is domain-aligned and predictable — a new engineer can infer the purpose of each package without reading code.
API layer handler classes are consistently package-private with a single public entry point (WordSketchApiServer), making the public surface small and coherent.
ExploreResponseAssembler provides a clear, single-responsibility translation layer between model and presentation.
model/package-info.java documents cross-cutting naming conventions explicitly, making the package self-describing.
GrammarConfig / GrammarConfigLoader separation gives consumers a data-only dependency without I/O coupling.
high_level_elegance.issue_character: The main structural weakness is documentation-level ownership drift: internal class names leak into model-layer docs, and the RelationType enum serves two semantic roles without acknowledging the distinction, creating subtle ambiguity about what the model contract actually guarantees.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase achieves solid domain-aligned decomposition and consistent public API surfaces, placing it above average on this dimension. It falls short of a higher score primarily because the model boundary is slightly undermined by internal implementation details surfacing in Javadoc and the RelationType enum's dual role blurring the model-vs-config contract.

strengths: Java naming conventions (camelCase, PascalCase, ALL_CAPS) are applied consistently with no cross-convention drift.
All API handler classes use the same package-private visibility pattern — no public leakage.
The mixed record/class strategy in the model package is explicitly documented in package-info.java, making it an intentional policy rather than ad-hoc variation.
Record usage for options types (ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions) is consistent with the model package's record policy.
convention_outlier.issue_character: Convention outliers are narrow and mostly behavioral rather than naming: one handler deviates from the validated-request pattern due to an early-exit check, and stale documentation references a renamed implementation detail.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates strong naming and structural consistency, placing it well above the midpoint for this dimension. The small deviations (double-parse in one handler, stale Javadoc constant reference, minor method-naming inconsistency in Main) are limited in scope and impact, justifying a score in the low-80s rather than the mid-70s.

strengths: wrapWithErrorHandling provides a uniform IAE→400, IOException→500 mapping applied to every API route — no handler needs to implement its own error response logic.
BlackLabQueryExecutor consistently wraps adapter errors as IOException, preserving context at the integration boundary.
GrammarConfigLoader uses descriptive IOException with specific error messages for all config validation failures.
SemanticFieldExplorer uses IllegalArgumentException consistently for precondition violations.
error_consistency.issue_character: The main inconsistency is a split between IAE-throw (for validation failures in the API layer) and null-return-after-self-handling (in resolveRelationConfig) within the same handler class, combined with a sentinel-return in LogDiceCalculator that diverges from the exception style used everywhere else.
error_consistency.score_rationale: Error handling is thoughtfully designed in the core pathways, with a clear top-level uniform wrapper and consistent IAE-throw for validation. Two localized deviations — the null-return pattern in resolveRelationConfig and the NaN sentinel in LogDiceCalculator — are the primary reasons the score does not reach the mid-80s. These are real inconsistencies that a developer maintaining the codebase would need to recognise and mentally switch between.

strengths: API handler method names consistently express HTTP endpoint semantics without leaking implementation vocabulary.
Domain terms (collocate, lemma, logDice, sketch, relation) are used uniformly throughout the codebase.
Record and class names in the model package are descriptive and noun-phrase based: DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult.
Parameter names in public APIs are consistently meaningful: seeds, minLogDice, topCollocates, nounsPerCollocate.
naming_quality.issue_character: Naming weaknesses are concentrated in a small set of helper/utility method names that use generic or abbreviated terms (round2dp, buildRawAdjectiveProfiles, explore in MultiSeedExplorer) where more specific domain vocabulary is available.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The codebase shows strong naming discipline at the API-facing and model levels. The score is held back from the high-80s by a cluster of weaker names in exploration internals and the ExploreResponseAssembler utility method — not systematic problems, but enough individual cases to indicate room for improvement.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface is honest about its single-implementation status and deferred-split rationale is documented.
SemanticFieldExplorer facade provides genuine value as a unified exploration API over two collaborating classes.
GrammarConfig/GrammarConfigLoader is a well-fitted separation with no unnecessary indirection.
CqlUtils and LongIntHashMap are cohesive utilities that justify their existence.
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: The primary issue is presentation logic (Edge.toMap, pattern-building in RelationConfig) leaking into value objects, and a few pass-through delegates (parseBaseExploreRequest, wrapHandler) that add indirection without adding value.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: Abstractions in this codebase generally pay for themselves — the interface, facade, and utility patterns are justified and the Javadoc honestly documents the design decisions. The score is held back by Edge.toMap embedding serialisation in the model, RelationConfig doing pattern analysis beyond data storage, and two pass-through wrappers that increase call depth without benefit.

strengths: No duplicate libraries for JSON, logging, or testing — the dependency set is lean and purposeful.
All dependency versions are explicitly pinned via properties in pom.xml — reproducible builds.
The choice to use com.sun.net.httpserver avoids adding an HTTP server framework dependency.
Both BlackLab artifacts are co-versioned at 4.0.0 — no version skew between engine and query parser.
dependency_health.issue_character: The primary dependency health risk is external: the BlackLab resolution URL points to a raw GitHub repository content path that has no SLA guarantees, creating a latent CI fragility that cannot be mitigated by code changes alone.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The dependency profile is lean and well-managed for a JVM NLP project of this scope, placing it solidly above average. The GitHub raw-content Maven repository is the single significant health concern — not a bug, but a fragility that could break CI builds without code changes, and the documented mitigation steps are optional rather than enforced.

strengths: MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator both use explicit, well-named phase decomposition that makes the algorithm readable at the orchestration level.
LongIntHashMap is a correctly implemented and well-documented specialized data structure.
ExploreResponseAssembler uses consistent extraction granularity with no micro-fragmentation or monolith methods.
CqlUtils demonstrates clean, well-tested string manipulation with balanced helper extraction.
low_level_elegance.issue_character: The main low-level issues are in ExplorationHandlers: verbose local-variable unpacking from a record, redundant query-string parsing, and a null-return-after-side-effect pattern that adds repetitive null-check boilerplate at multiple call sites.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: Function and class internals are generally clean and intention-revealing across the codebase. ExplorationHandlers contains a cluster of low-level friction points that affect reading flow — none are bugs, but together they represent a quality gap compared to the cleaner orchestration in the exploration layer. The score reflects solid work with localized rough edges.

strengths: ExploreResponseAssembler provides a single, explicit translation boundary between model and presentation — no duplicated assembly logic across handlers.
ExplorationOptions/SingleSeedExplorationOptions cleanly model the input contract at the API-to-exploration seam.
Handler classes each have a well-defined integration role: parsing, delegating to domain layer, assembling response — no tangled back-and-forth.
The api→exploration→query dependency chain is one-directional with no circular call patterns.
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: The seam issues concentrate around ExplorationResult being semantically overloaded (same type for single and multi-seed results with different seed field semantics) and ExplorationOptions being destructured at the final delegation point rather than threaded through intact.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The layering is clean and most handoffs are explicit and minimal. Two localized seam issues — the implicit comma-joined seed encoding in ExplorationResult and the error message from a domain method leaking an internal class name to API clients — are genuine mid-level design gaps that prevent a higher score. The options-destructuring pattern is a minor awkwardness, not a serious coupling issue.

strengths: No noisy entry/exit logging — logger.debug is used only for meaningful exploration milestones and progress counters.
Error handling is centralized in wrapWithErrorHandling — no identical boilerplate error blocks copied across files.
Class-level Javadoc in SemanticFieldExplorer, HttpApiUtils, and GrammarConfigLoader provides genuine insight into design decisions, not just code restatements.
No defensive null-checks on typed-non-nullable values; jspecify annotations carry the nullability contract without redundant guards.
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: AI-generated-debt patterns are minor and localized to a small set of accessor Javadoc entries that restate the method name or return type, and one comment that makes an inaccurate structural claim.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase is largely free of restating comments, defensive overengineering, and boilerplate duplication — strong indicators of deliberate, edited code. The score reflects this positively. The small number of trivial accessor Javadocs and one inaccurate comment keep it from reaching the high-80s, as these are exactly the pattern (comment echoes code) that this dimension targets.

strengths: Major renames (BcqlHandlers→CorpusQueryHandlers, ExploreOptions→ExplorationOptions) are fully propagated with no stale call-sites.
Deprecated constructors have been removed from SemanticFieldExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator.
The 'limit' alias shim is narrowly scoped (one if-branch) and clearly documented with a removal plan.
The tag= legacy attribute path is documented as having no known active callers, reducing risk.
incomplete_migration.issue_character: Both remaining migration artifacts are documented backward-compat shims in single locations, with no spread of old-API call-sites — the migration is structurally complete but has two low-risk residual compatibility paths.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: The codebase is in very good shape for this dimension: no deprecated symbols are called by active production logic, no stale TODO markers reference obsolete APIs, and the recent rename wave left no straggler usages. The two remaining shims (limit alias, tag= fallback) are single-location, documented, and have explicit removal notes. This places the score comfortably above the midpoint — the only gap from a high score is the absence of a concrete removal plan or version gate for these shims.

strengths: Seven focused packages with clear domain names — api, config, exploration, indexer, model, query, utils, viz.
No package exceeds 10 files (largest is model/ with 10 including package-info).
Coupling direction is one-way (api→exploration→query→utils); no circular package-level dependencies.
Recent moves (QueryResults to query/, RadialPlot to viz/) improved placement.
The indexer/blacklab/ sub-package correctly isolates the BlackLab-specific implementation.
package_organization.issue_character: The primary issue is the api/ package mixing HTTP infrastructure, HTTP handlers, and response assembly into one flat directory, and a stale empty 'collections/' directory that adds noise.
package_organization.score_rationale: The package structure is above average for this codebase size — domain packages are well-named and focused, coupling direction is clean, and no package is grossly overloaded. The score is held below 80 by the api/ package's three-concern co-location (server infra + handlers + response assembly) and the dead empty 'collections/' directory. A developer navigating the api/ package must understand which files are routing vs. handling vs. assembling, which is mildly taxing but not severely disorienting.

strengths: Java's compiler enforces all return type contracts — no annotation-lie return types possible.
QueryExecutor interface has complete, accurate Javadoc for all 7 methods.
The nullable-return-means-400-already-sent convention in validateExploreRequest is explicitly documented.
The dual-mechanism in parseBcqlRequest (null for 413, IAE for 400) is clearly documented.
The testing-seam package-private overload of exploreByPattern is clearly labeled and discouraged for production use.
contract_coherence.issue_character: The main contract issues are a misleading method name (requireNextArg calling System.exit) and an undocumented double-parse side-effect in handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti — both are localized and low-severity.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates strong contract discipline overall: Java's static typing handles the structural contracts, and the handful of unusual conventions (nullable sentinel returns, testing seams) are explicitly documented. The score is held below 80 by the requireNextArg naming mismatch and the undocumented double-parse side-effect, which create minor misleading signals for developers reading call-sites. Neither is a correctness bug — they are honesty gaps in the documented contracts.

strengths: Control flow in exploration algorithms is well-structured with clear phase separation (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates).
Early-return guard clauses are used consistently (empty lemma checks, empty seed checks).
The executeRelationQueries method uses explicit Predicate and BiFunction parameters, making conditional filtering logic explicit rather than scattered.
Boolean conditions are direct and unambiguous throughout handlers.
logic_clarity.issue_character: The main logic clarity issues are a single unreachable defensive guard (Math.max(1, freq) where freq>=2) and the double-parse of query string in one handler — both are minor clarity issues without correctness impact.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The codebase shows solid logic discipline: no dead branches after unconditional returns, no always-true/false conditions, and no async-without-await patterns. The unreachable Math.max guard and the double-parse create modest clarity noise. The silent acceptance of a comma-separated value as a single seed in handleSemanticFieldExplore (no validation, no splitting) is the most user-visible logic ambiguity. Overall these are small issues in an otherwise clear control flow.

strengths: Java's compiler enforces all method signature types — no return-type annotation lies are structurally possible.
Domain enums (RelationType, RelationEdgeType, PosGroup) are used consistently; no enum-bypass raw string comparisons found.
Model layer uses Java records with well-typed fields (AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge).
QueryResults nested records provide type-safe result containers for all three query modes.
type_safety.issue_character: The primary type safety concerns are the magic string literals for adjective categories (duplicating enum semantics without a backing enum) and the inconsistent application of nullability annotations on public API surfaces.
type_safety.score_rationale: Java's static typing provides a strong baseline — no annotation-lie returns, no raw types, no unchecked casts are visible. The score is held below 80 by: (1) the magic string literals for adjective category serialization, which duplicate AdjectiveProfile boolean semantics without compile-time safety; (2) inconsistent @Nullable/@NonNull coverage on public parameters; and (3) the Map<String,Object> response pattern, which is conventional but sacrifices response-shape verifiability. These are common Java patterns rather than egregious issues, but they represent real type discipline gaps.

strengths: SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries with callback parameters is a clean data-driven design that eliminates structural duplication between surface and dependency sketches.
SemanticFieldExplorer as a facade with package-private delegates (MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator) cleanly enforces the exploration sub-domain boundary.
The ValidatedExploreRequest private record prevents parameter telescoping in the shared exploration handler path.
CorpusQueryHandlers cleanly separates request parsing (parseBcqlRequest) from execution (handleCorpusQuery).
design_coherence.issue_character: The main design coherence issue is ExplorationHandlers using three different validation styles across four handlers — two use the validateExploreRequest shared path, one (comparison) does manual validation, one (examples) does its own parsing — creating a fragmented pattern in a single class.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase has strong design coherence in the query and exploration layers, with clean facades, focused classes, and data-driven patterns. The score is held below 80 by ExplorationHandlers' fragmented handler patterns — the shared validate→dispatch path covers only 2 of 4 handlers, the double-parse is a structural artifact of adding a pre-check without refactoring the shared path, and handleSemanticFieldComparison bypasses the shared validation entirely. These are cohesion issues within a single class, not cross-cutting structural problems.

strengths: getCorsAllowOrigin() explicitly defers system property read to call time — avoids stale snapshot.
WordSketchApiServer separates construction from port binding (start()) — testable without network binding.
AtomicReference<WordSketchApiServer> for shutdown hook ensures cleanup even on construction failure.
No global mutable singletons in production code — all dependencies are constructor-injected.
GrammarConfigLoader is a stateless static utility, avoiding singleton initialization order issues.
initialization_coupling.issue_character: No significant initialization coupling issues found — the codebase consistently uses constructor injection and defers I/O-bound initialization to start() or explicit open() calls.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: This codebase achieves a high score on initialization coupling: no import-time side effects, no module-level globals with construction-order dependencies, no circular init dependencies, and explicit lifecycle management (WordSketchApiServer.start()/stop(), BlackLabQueryExecutor.close()). The minor gap from a perfect score is that getCorsAllowOrigin() reads a system property on every request (intentional, documented) which makes the effective 'initialization' dynamic rather than settled at startup — a low-severity semantic issue.

strengths: Core domain logic (exploration algorithms, logDice scoring, CQL parsing, grammar config loading) is well covered by unit tests using stub/mock executors.
No snapshot test overuse — tests use explicit assertions.
Live-index tests are correctly gated with Assumptions.assumeTrue rather than permanently disabled.
SemanticFieldExplorerInternalTest accesses package-private seams correctly via package co-location.
test_strategy.issue_character: The gap is at the integration boundary: the HTTP layer (WordSketchApiServer route registration, VisualizationHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers HTTP path) and the live-index execution path (BlackLabQueryExecutor against a real corpus) are systematically skipped in CI, leaving two important entry points without automated coverage.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The test suite is solid for domain logic — stub-based unit tests cover exploration, config loading, CQL parsing, and scoring. The score is held below 70 by two structural gaps: (1) WordSketchApiServer's route registration and HTTP infrastructure are never directly tested, meaning a registration error would not be caught in CI; (2) the live-index tests, which validate the actual BlackLabQueryExecutor execution path, are silently skipped in all environments without a corpus index, creating a CI blind spot for the most complex production code path. These are missing critical-path tests, not low-value gaps.

strengths: All exploration endpoints use the same 'relation', 'top', 'min_logdice', 'min_shared' parameter names consistently.
POST /api/bcql uses a JSON body with 'query' and 'top' — appropriate for complex patterns that would be awkward as query params.
QueryExecutor interface methods have consistent (lemma, pattern, minLogDice, maxResults) parameter ordering.
RelationEdgeType enum provides stable JSON serialization labels ('seed_adj', 'discovered_adj', 'modifier').
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The REST API surface has a fragmented naming vocabulary: 'seeds' is overloaded across three endpoints with different cardinality semantics, 'word1'/'word2' contradict the 'seeds' convention, and 'adjective'/'noun' in examples add a third naming pattern — a developer using multiple endpoints must track three different naming conventions for the same concept.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The Java API layer (QueryExecutor, SemanticFieldExplorer) is relatively coherent with consistent parameter ordering and documented interface contracts. The REST API layer scores significantly lower: three different naming conventions for lemma inputs ('seeds', 'word1'/'word2', 'adjective'/'noun'), the 'seeds' parameter overloaded with different cardinality semantics across three endpoints, and an endpoint path (/api/semantic-field) that does not signal its comparative function. These are concrete navigability issues for API clients integrating multiple endpoints.

strengths: Loopback binding (InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress()) applied uniformly — consistent network-level access control for all endpoints.
No per-route auth decorator present on some routes and absent on others — zero inconsistency in auth treatment.
CORS policy applied uniformly via setCorsHeader on every response method — no per-endpoint CORS differences.
No user data stored or processed — authorization is not required by the data model.
authorization_consistency.issue_character: There are no authorization inconsistencies — the design is uniformly unauthenticated by intent for a local research tool. The only gap is documentation: the loopback-only deployment assumption is not stated in a README warning or in the server's Javadoc.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: This is a local research tool where all endpoints are consistently and intentionally unauthenticated — no route has auth that its siblings lack, no RLS is applied inconsistently, and the loopback binding provides a uniform trust boundary. The score is held just below 90 because: (1) the /api/bcql endpoint accepts arbitrary corpus queries with no rate limiting — benign when loopback-only but would require auth gating if the server were ever exposed beyond localhost; (2) the loopback-only deployment assumption is not explicitly documented in server code or README, making the security model implicit rather than stated.

issues.summary: RelationType enum conflates structural query strategy (SURFACE, DEP) with semantic relation categories (ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF)
issues.suggestion: Split into two enums: QueryStrategy { SURFACE, DEP } for dispatch in SketchHandlers, and SemanticRelationType { ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF } for semantic classification in config and exploration. RelationConfig can carry both where applicable.
evidence: SketchHandlers uses RelationType.SURFACE and RelationType.DEP as dispatch selectors for query strategy.
CollocateProfileComparator.deriveAdjectivePattern calls RelationUtils.findBestCollocatePattern with RelationType.ADJ_MODIFIER, RelationType.ADJ_PREDICATE — semantic categories.
SemanticFieldExplorer.deriveNounCqlConstraint uses RelationType.OBJECT_OF, RelationType.SUBJECT_OF — further semantic categories.
GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelationType parses any RelationType value from JSON, making the config file API depend on the same mixed enum.

issues.summary: All api-layer handler classes import com.sun.net.httpserver — an unsupported internal JDK API — creating a codebase-wide dependency on a non-guaranteed namespace
issues.suggestion: Accept the risk with a documented decision comment in WordSketchApiServer, or wrap the HttpExchange dependency behind a thin interface (e.g., RequestContext) to allow future migration. At minimum, add a pom.xml comment documenting the conscious choice to use this JDK-internal HTTP server.
evidence: All six api handler files begin with import com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpExchange;
com.sun.net.httpserver is not part of the Java SE public API specification — it may be restricted or encapsulated in future JDK releases.
The coupling is bounded to the api package and does not leak into exploration or query layers.

issues.summary: ComparisonResult Javadoc references internal CollocateProfileComparator rather than the public SemanticFieldExplorer façade
issues.suggestion: Update ComparisonResult Javadoc to reference SemanticFieldExplorer#compareCollocateProfiles (the public API) rather than the package-private CollocateProfileComparator.
evidence: ComparisonResult.java line 11: 'Produced by CollocateProfileComparator#compareCollocateProfiles' — CollocateProfileComparator is package-private in exploration.
The public API for comparison is SemanticFieldExplorer#compareCollocateProfiles, which is what callers (ExplorationHandlers) use.
This means the model's documentation couples it to an internal implementation class rather than the publicly declared boundary.

issues.summary: RelationType enum in model conflates structural dispatch values (SURFACE, DEP) with semantic category values (ADJ_PREDICATE etc.) without documented separation
issues.suggestion: Add enum-level Javadoc that explicitly documents the two groups (query strategy discriminators vs. semantic relation categories), or split into two enums: QueryStrategy and SemanticRelationType.
evidence: RelationType.java contains: SURFACE, DEP (structural), ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF (semantic) — no enum comment separates these two groups.

CollocateProfileComparator and SemanticFieldExplorer use ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF as semantic filter criteria.
No documentation at the enum level communicates that these are two distinct conceptual groups.

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti parses query params twice, deviating from the single-validation pattern used by sibling handlers

handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleSemanticFieldComparison, and handleSemanticFieldExamples do not call parseBaseExploreRequest directly — they use validateExploreRequest or the params map obtained from it.

issues.summary: HttpApiUtils Javadoc references a non-existent constant CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN instead of the actual getCorsAllowOrigin() method

issues.summary: resolveRelationConfig in ExplorationHandlers sends a 400 response and returns null, mixing self-handled errors with the IAE-throw convention used by sibling methods
issues.suggestion: Replace the null-return pattern with a dedicated exception (e.g. a package-private UnknownRelationException extending IllegalArgumentException) or use IAE directly. Let wrapWithErrorHandling send the 400 response, eliminating the null checks in callers.

requireParam in the same call chain throws IllegalArgumentException which propagates automatically to wrapWithErrorHandling — no caller null check needed.
The two error-handling patterns coexist within the same class, requiring developers to understand which methods self-handle and which throw.

issues.summary: LogDiceCalculator.compute returns Double.NaN (UNCOMPUTABLE) for invalid inputs instead of throwing IllegalArgumentException, inconsistent with the IAE-for-validation pattern used elsewhere
issues.suggestion: Document clearly in the class Javadoc that this is an intentional NaN-sentinel design (e.g., because callers aggregate many scores and need to filter rather than abort), OR switch to throwing IllegalArgumentException on truly invalid inputs (zero/negative freq) and reserving NaN only for the score-is-uncomputable-but-inputs-are-technically-valid case.

All other computation-boundary validations in this codebase throw IllegalArgumentException (requireParam, exploreByPattern preconditions).

issues.summary: validateExploreRequest parses, resolves, and constructs a ValidatedExploreRequest — the name implies validation-only but the method has significant construction responsibility
issues.suggestion: Rename to parseExploreRequest or buildValidatedExploreRequest to communicate that the method both validates and constructs the request context.

A developer reading the call site 'ValidatedExploreRequest req = validateExploreRequest(exchange)' must check the implementation to understand what is being returned and when null is returned.
'validate' conventionally implies a side-effect-free boolean or exception-throw check, not construction of a result object.

issues.summary: round2dp in ExploreResponseAssembler uses an abbreviation inconsistent with the codebase's full-word naming style

issues.summary: MultiSeedExplorer.explore is overly generic for a method that computes a specific multi-seed collocate intersection
issues.suggestion: Rename to exploreSeeds or computeCollocateIntersection to make the multi-seed nature explicit and distinguish it from single-seed exploration.

issues.summary: Edge.toMap() puts JSON serialization logic in the model class, which should be a pure value object free of presentation concerns
issues.suggestion: Move Edge.toMap() into a private helper in ExploreResponseAssembler (e.g. serializeEdge(Edge e)). Edge becomes a pure record. RelationEdgeType.label() can stay as a stable domain label used for both display and serialization.

ExploreResponseAssembler.populateExploreResponse calls 'edges.stream().map(Edge::toMap).toList()' — the assembler delegates serialisation back to the model.

Moving toMap() into ExploreResponseAssembler would make Edge a pure value object and centralise all model→JSON translation in the assembler.

issues.summary: RelationConfig builds and parses CQL patterns (buildFullPattern, buildCollocateReversePattern, computeCollocatePosGroup) — structural analysis and construction beyond data storage responsibility
issues.suggestion: Extract pattern-building and POS inference into a companion class (e.g. RelationPatternBuilder) or into static methods in GrammarConfigLoader/RelationUtils. RelationConfig becomes a leaner data record storing pattern, positions, and already-computed POS group.
evidence: RelationConfig.buildFullPattern(String seed) and buildFullPattern(String head, String collocate) use CqlUtils.substituteAtPosition to construct full CQL patterns from the stored template.
RelationConfig.buildCollocateReversePattern extracts the collocate-position token from the pattern string.
RelationConfig.computeCollocatePosGroup parses the collocate token's xpos constraint to infer the POS group.

All three methods do string analysis that is closer to parsing/construction logic than to data property access. This logic could live in a companion class (RelationPatternBuilder) or in GrammarConfigLoader.

issues.summary: ExplorationHandlers.parseBaseExploreRequest is a pure pass-through to HttpApiUtils.parseQueryParams with no added behavior
issues.suggestion: Remove parseBaseExploreRequest and call HttpApiUtils.parseQueryParams(exchange.getRequestURI().getQuery()) directly at each call site, or fold it into validateExploreRequest.
evidence: parseBaseExploreRequest(HttpExchange exchange): returns HttpApiUtils.parseQueryParams(exchange.getRequestURI().getQuery()) — single-line body.

issues.summary: BlackLab 4.x is resolved from a GitHub raw-content URL with no SLA — a CI fragility that can break builds without any code change

The pom.xml comment explicitly warns: 'This URL has no SLA and can become unavailable (rate-limited, branch deleted, or repo renamed).'

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldExplore unpacks ValidatedExploreRequest into 7 separate local variables before any logic, inflating method length unnecessarily
issues.suggestion: Access record fields inline at usage sites rather than unpacking them into local variables, or extract a single well-named local variable for the params sub-record if reuse justifies it. Alternatively, keep only the few fields that are used more than twice.
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExplore: String seed = req.seedsRaw(); RelationConfig resolvedConfig = req.relationConfig(); String relationType = resolvedConfig.relationType()... int topCollocates = req.exploreParams().topCollocates(); int minShared = req.exploreParams().minShared(); double minLogDice = req.exploreParams().minLogDice(); int nounsPerCollocate = req.exploreParams().nounsPerCollocate(); — seven declarations before any logic.
The record fields and nested record fields could be accessed inline at each usage site, reducing visual noise in this relatively short method.

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti parses the request URI query twice — once explicitly and once inside validateExploreRequest
issues.suggestion: Extract the nouns_per check into validateExploreRequest itself, or pass the already-parsed params map into validateExploreRequest to eliminate the redundant parse. The simplest fix is to check params.containsKey('nouns_per') after validateExploreRequest using req.params().

Then: ValidatedExploreRequest req = validateExploreRequest(exchange); — which internally calls parseBaseExploreRequest(exchange) again.

issues.summary: resolveRelationConfig sends a 400 HTTP response and returns null, forcing three callers to add identical null-check-and-return boilerplate
issues.suggestion: Throw IllegalArgumentException from resolveRelationConfig instead of sending the response and returning null. The wrapWithErrorHandling wrapper will catch IAE and send the 400 response automatically. This eliminates the null checks in callers and makes the method side-effect-free.
evidence: resolveRelationConfig: calls sendError(exchange, 400, ...) and returns null on two error paths.

The null-check-and-return pattern appears three times as a direct consequence of the side-effecting null-return design.

issues.summary: ExplorationResult.seed field carries a single lemma in single-seed mode and a comma-joined string in multi-seed mode — implicit format contract undocumented at the boundary

issues.summary: The 400 error for compareCollocateProfiles with < 2 seeds exposes an internal domain class name in the API response message

issues.summary: GrammarConfig accessor Javadoc entries restate the method name rather than adding insight

issues.summary: @Nullable/@NonNull from jspecify used in 3 locations but absent from most public API parameters

issues.summary: getCorsAllowOrigin() re-reads system property on every HTTP response, allowing CORS origin to change mid-runtime

evidence: GET /api/semantic-field maps to handleSemanticFieldComparison — computes pairwise adjective profile comparisons.
The URL path suggests a 'semantic field' resource overview, not a comparison operation.

The endpoint family: /api/semantic-field, /api/semantic-field/explore, /api/semantic-field/explore-multi, /api/semantic-field/examples — the root path does not follow the sub-resource naming pattern of its siblings.

issues.summary: compareCollocateProfiles Java API does not accept a RelationConfig, while all other exploration methods do — asymmetric interface
issues.suggestion: Add a RelationConfig overload to compareCollocateProfiles so callers can specify the comparison relation: `compareCollocateProfiles(Set<String> seedNouns, RelationConfig relation, ExplorationOptions opts)`. Pass the relation's collocate pattern to CollocateProfileComparator rather than using the hard-coded adjective pattern. This symmetrizes the Java API surface with the other two exploration methods.
evidence: exploreByPattern(seed, RelationConfig, SingleSeedExplorationOptions) — takes relation.
exploreMultiSeed(seeds, RelationConfig, ExplorationOptions) — takes relation.
compareCollocateProfiles(seedNouns, ExplorationOptions) — NO relation param; uses hard-coded adjective pattern from grammar config.
Callers cannot select which grammatical relation to use for comparison — fixed to adjective modifier/predicate.

issues.summary: Server loopback-only security model is not documented — a future deployment change could unintentionally expose all endpoints without auth
issues.suggestion: Add a Javadoc note to WordSketchApiServer.start() and to the README: 'This server binds to loopback only. All endpoints are unauthenticated — do not expose this server on a network interface without adding authentication middleware.' This documents the security contract so that a future change from loopback to a network address triggers a deliberate security review.
evidence: start() uses InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress() — correct for local-only use.
No comment in start() or class Javadoc explaining that this loopback binding is the security boundary and that adding network binding would require auth.
The /api/bcql endpoint accepts arbitrary corpus queries — in a public deployment this would need rate-limiting and query complexity controls beyond the current size/bracket limits.
